title: Isaiah 20
subtitle: Isaiah Walks Naked as a Sign

sections.header: Verses 1–3 — “Go, Strip Off the Sackcloth and Take Off Your Sandals”

heb.paragraph: "Go, strip off the sackcloth from your body and take off your sandals." And he did so, going around stripped (ʿārōm) and barefoot. Then the LORD said: "Just as my servant Isaiah has gone stripped and barefoot for three years, as a sign and portent against Egypt and Cush" (vv.2–3). In the year the Assyrian army commander came to Ashdod and captured it (711 BC), God commands Isaiah to walk naked for three years as a living sign that Egypt and Cush will be led away as captives.

refs.note: David wearing a linen ephod was appropriate ritual clothing, not an example of nakedness or minimal clothing
Therefore I will weep and wail; I will go about barefoot and naked — Micah’s parallel sign-act.
The linen belt buried at the Euphrates — another extreme prophetic sign-act.
Ezekiel’s 430 days lying on his side — the most extreme sign-act in the OT, paralleling Isaiah’s 3 years.

notes.note: The year Ashdod fell (711 BC). The historical marker grounds the sign-act in real time. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

Calvin reads the nakedness as prophetic theatre: God uses Isaiah’s body to communicate what words alone cannot convey. The embarrassment is the point — it matches the embarrassment Egypt’s allies will feel.
Calvin notes the THREE YEARS: this is not a moment of ecstasy but a sustained commitment. Isaiah lived the message every day for 1,095 days.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET confirms the Ashdod campaign: Sargon II’s inscriptions record the conquest of Ashdod in 711 BC. The date is archaeologically secure.
The NET notes "stripped" (ʿārōm) may mean completely naked or wearing only a loincloth. Either way, the exposure is a sign of prisoner-of-war humiliation.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt reads the sign-act as the most visceral anti-Egypt argument in Isaiah: words were not enough, so God commands embodied prophecy. The prophet’s body IS the sermon.
Oswalt: three years is the longest prophetic sign-act attributed to Isaiah. Ezekiel’s 430 days (Ezek 4) is longer but different in kind. Isaiah’s sign was public and daily.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the sign-act as the canonical bridge between the Egypt oracle (ch.19) and the Babylon/Edom/Arabia oracles (ch.21): the prophet’s body interrupts the literary sequence with lived theology.
Childs notes the sign-act functions like a parable-in-action: it arrests attention and demands interpretation. The naked prophet FORCES the question: why?

hist.context: The date marker (the fall of Ashdod, 711 BC) is historically confirmed by Sargon II’s own records. The three-year naked prophecy is the most extreme prophetic sign-act in Isaiah. The nakedness represents the humiliation of prisoners of war, who were stripped and marched barefoot. Isaiah embodies the fate of those who trust in Egypt: they will be stripped, exposed, ashamed. The sign is directed against Judah’s temptation to ally with Egypt, not against Egypt itself.

refs.note: Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help and do not look to the Holy One of Israel — the same anti-alliance theology.
Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help, who rely on horses — the repeated warning.
How shall we escape if we ignore so great a salvation? — the NT version of the escape-question.
Some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD our God.

notes.note: Egypt and Cush led away stripped. The sign-act becomes reality: what Isaiah modelled, Egypt will experience.
Those who trusted in Egypt will be dismayed. The alliance that promised safety delivers shame.
"How then can we escape?" The question that should drive Judah to God instead of to Egypt.
Calvin applies the lesson broadly: every human alliance that replaces trust in God will fail. The question "how can we escape?" is answered not by finding a better ally but by returning to the LORD.
Calvin: the rhetorical question is both devastating and hopeful. Devastating because Egypt cannot save; hopeful because the question opens the door to the only answer: God.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes Assyrian reliefs from Nineveh and Nimrud depict exactly the scene described: prisoners of war stripped naked and marched in procession. The description is historically documented.

The NET observes "coastland" (iy) typically refers to Mediterranean coastal settlements. The people asking "how can we escape?" are Judah’s allies and neighbours who also relied on Egypt’s protection.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt reads the rhetorical question as the chapter’s theological centre: the collapse of human security-systems creates the space for divine trust. Every failed alliance is an invitation to faith.
Oswalt: "how then can we escape?" implies its own answer: you cannot escape by looking horizontally (to Egypt). You can only escape by looking vertically (to God).

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the oracle as a canonical parable on misplaced trust: the nations-oracles section repeatedly warns against alliances that bypass God. The pattern is consistent from ch.7 (Ahaz trusting Assyria) through ch.20 (Judah trusting Egypt).
Childs notes the question "how can we escape?" remains open in Isaiah — it is answered fully only in Second Isaiah’s proclamation of the Servant who provides the escape (ch.53).

hist.context: The rhetorical question "How can we escape?" (v.6) is the oracle’s payoff: if Egypt (the superpower you trusted) cannot save itself, how will it save you? The answer is: it cannot. The only escape is trust in God, not in military alliances. The "buttocks bared" detail (v.4) is not gratuitous but historically accurate: Assyrian reliefs depict prisoners marched naked as humiliation.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVIn that day the people who live on this coast will say, \u2018See what has happened to those we relied on, those we fled to for help and deliverance from the king of Assyria! How then can we escape?\u2019 \u201dESVAnd the inhabitants of this coastland will say in that day, \u2018Behold, this is what has happened to those in whom we hoped and to whom we fled for help to be delivered from the king of Assyria! And we, how shall we escape?\u2019\u201dNIVIn the year that the supreme commander, sent by Sargon king of Assyria, came to Ashdod and attacked and captured it\u2014ESVIn the year that the commander in chief, who was sent by Sargon the king of Assyria, came to Ashdod and fought against it and captured it\u2014

src.title: The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BC)
src.quote: The Cyrus Cylinder relates to Deutero-Isaiah (chapters 40-55), not Isaiah chapter 1 being discussed here.
src.note: The cylinder confirms Isaiah’s prophecy of a Persian king who would liberate captives and authorize temple rebuilding, whether the prophecy is dated early (8th c.) or late (6th c.).

src.title: The Sennacherib Prism (c.700 BC)
src.quote: The Sennacherib Prism relates to events described in Isaiah 36-39, not Isaiah chapter 1.
src.note: The silence confirms Isaiah 36–37: Sennacherib besieged but never conquered Jerusalem. His own records inadvertently verify Isaiah’s narrative of divine deliverance.

src.title: The Great Isaiah Scroll (1QIsaᵃ, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A complete copy of Isaiah found at Qumran, dated c.125 BC. The oldest complete biblical manuscript, it is virtually identical to the Masoretic text 1,000 years later.
src.note: The scroll demonstrates the remarkable textual stability of Isaiah across a millennium. The text the church reads today is essentially the text the Qumran community read.

rec.text: "The year Ashdod fell (711 BC). The historical marker grounds the sign-act in real time."

rows.label: How Can We Escape? — 20:4–6
rows.text: Egypt led away naked. Those who trusted: DISMAYED. "How then can we ESCAPE?"

hebtext.note: "Go, strip off the sackcloth from your body and take off your sandals." And he did so, going around stripped (ʿārōm) and barefoot. Then the LORD said: "Just as my servant Isaiah has gone stripped and barefoot for three years, as a sign and portent against Egypt and Cush" (vv.2–3). In the year the Assyrian army commander came to Ashdod and captured it (711 BC), God commands Isaiah to walk naked for three years as a living sign that Egypt and Cush will be led away as captives.

thread.anchor: Isaiah Walks Naked as a Sign
thread.text: David's dancing in 2 Sam 6:14 involved wearing a linen ephod (priestly garment), not nakedness. This is worship, not prophetic symbolism like Isaiah's sign-act.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks: the most extreme sign-act. Faith at 7: the anti-alliance message drives toward trust in God alone.

coaching.tip: God commands Isaiah to walk naked and barefoot for three years (v. 3) — a prophetic sign-act representing the humiliation awaiting Egypt and Cush. This isn't performance art; it's embodied prophecy at its most costly. Ezekiel will later lie on his side for over a year (Ezekiel 4). Hosea marries a promiscuous woman (Hosea 1). The prophets don't just speak the word — they live it, at enormous personal cost.

'How then can we escape?' (v. 6). The chapter ends with Judah's allies exposed as unreliable. If Egypt and Cush fall to Assyria, what hope has tiny Judah? The question hangs in the air without a direct answer — but Isaiah has already given it: Immanuel (7:14), the Branch (4:2), the child on David's throne (9:6). The political chapters keep pointing back to the theological ones.

prayer_prompt: As Isaiah walks stripped and barefoot for three years as a living sign, consider asking God what inconvenient obedience He may be asking of you — and whether you are willing to look foolish for His sake.
